narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer despite the challenges many face in this field. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, an LSAT score of 24.50, top-decile rankings in Year 1 and Year 3 at your school, an upper-middle family income background, and plans to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated an 80% chance that you would fail the bar exam— a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which stood out as a strong negative signal compared to typical students who rank much higher on average. Your LSAT score was the next biggest driver, as it fell well below the dataset average of around 37, signaling potential struggles in the rigorous legal reasoning needed for the bar. Your Year 1 decile, while in the top tier, still contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction relative to peers. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income and undergraduate GPA—though below the typical 3.21 average—helped nudge the prediction slightly toward passing, as did your full-time enrollment plans, which align with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having strengths in school deciles and socioeconomic support, but the gaps in LSAT and later academic decile performance were the dominant hurdles here. This isn't a reflection of your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in academia, but rather how these metrics stacked up against historical bar passage patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year grades if possible, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students. Many have turned similar rejections into success by addressing these key areas—your full-time commitment and family support position you well for that journey. We're rooting for you to reapply stronger.
